summary: DeVault demonstrates a finger protocol client written in Hare, following up on an earlier io_uring finger server post. The implementation is remarkably concise thanks to Hare's new networking stack, particularly its net::dial module inspired by Go's net.Dial and Plan 9's dial function. The post highlights how Hare's dial design improves on Go by reintroducing the service parameter from Plan 9 and allowing third-party protocol handler extensions. The entire client fits in roughly 30 lines of code, handling DNS resolution, service lookup, and SRV records automatically.
key_points: Hare's net::dial module draws from both Go's net.Dial and Plan 9's dial function, combining ideas from both into a cleaner design
The service parameter reintroduced from Plan 9 enables automatic port resolution via internal service lists, /etc/services, and SRV DNS lookups
Unlike Go, Hare's dial is extensible by third-party libraries, allowing new protocol handlers for networks like AF_BLUETOOTH or AF_AX25
The entire finger client implementation is about 30 lines, demonstrating how good standard library design minimizes application code
money_quotes: Plan 9's "tcp!example.org!http" becomes net::dial("tcp", "example.org", "http") in our language — which we use to find the port (unless you specify it in the address).
Most programs written in our language will support SRV records with no additional effort.

Unlike Go, third-party libraries can define new protocol handlers for dial as well, perhaps opening it up in the future to networks like AF_BLUETOOTH, AF_AX25, and so on, complete with support for network-appropriate addresses and resolver functionality.
We just parse the command line, dial the server, write the username to it, and splice the connection into stdout. Much simpler than the server.
Expanding this with full support for all finger requests would probably only grow this code by 2 or 3 times.
key_insight: Good standard library design — particularly a well-thought-out dial abstraction inspired by Plan 9 — can make network client implementations trivially concise while automatically supporting features like SRV records and extensible protocol handlers.
